title: Romanos

body:
It Is Settled From God’s Perspective A Clear and Simple Walk Through the Message of Romans Author BJ Wry
Purpose of This Book This book was written to help readers understand a simple but often misunderstood truth:
What God has settled, does not need to be re-settled by us.
Many believers live as though their standing before God is fragile, uncertain, or dependent on their ongoing performance. The message of Romans presents a very different picture — one where God acts decisively, justly, and finally.
This book does not attempt to be technical or academic.
It is written in clear language, with careful thought, so that the reader can follow the flow of Paul’s argument without needing a background in theology.
Romans will be treated as a message to be understood, not merely verses to be quoted.
How to Use This Book Read slowly Follow the argument as it unfolds Let conclusions arise from the text itself Resist the urge to insert assumptions The goal is not to tell you what to think, but to help you see what the text is actually saying.

body:
Introduction Why “It Is Settled” Few phrases create more tension in the Christian life than the question:
“Am I secure before God?”
For many, assurance feels temporary. It rises and falls with obedience, emotions, spiritual disciplines, or recent failures. Even sincere believers often live with an unspoken fear that something could still go wrong.
The letter to the Romans confronts that fear directly.
Paul does not build his case on human faithfulness, but on God’s action, God’s righteousness, and God’s integrity (Romans 1:16–17). From the opening chapters, Romans presses the reader toward a single conclusion:
What God has declared righteous, He does not later place back into question.
A Common Struggle Many Christians hold two beliefs at the same time:
God justifies the sinner freely (Romans 3:24). The believer must now maintain that standing through effort. Romans does not allow these two ideas to coexist comfortably.
If righteousness is credited apart from works, then it is not sustained by works either (Romans 4:4–5). If peace with God is established by God’s action, then it cannot be undone by human weakness (Romans 5:1).
This tension is not theoretical — it affects how people pray, serve, obey, and even how they read Scripture.
Why Romans Matters Romans is not written to tell believers how to become spiritual.
It is written to explain what God has already done.
Paul’s argument unfolds carefully:
The universal problem of sin (Romans 1:18–3:20) The inability of law to resolve it (Romans 3:19–20) God’s decisive action in Christ (Romans 3:21–26) The certainty of justification (Romans 4:1–8) The implications of that certainty for life (Romans 5–8) This book follows that same flow, allowing Romans to speak on its own terms.
What This Book Is — and Is Not This book is:
An explanation of Romans in simple language Focused on clarity and flow Rooted in the text itself This book is not:
A theological debate A system of doctrines A manual for Christian performance The aim is understanding — because clarity produces rest, and rest produces fruit (Romans 6:22).
In the next chapter, we begin where Paul begins:
not with solutions, but with the problem that makes the solution necessary (Romans 1:18).

body:
Chapter 2 The Problem Romans Exposes Before Romans ever offers assurance, it insists on clarity.
Paul does not begin with comfort.
He begins with truth — and the truth is not flattering.
Romans does not attempt to convince the reader that they need improvement.
It insists that something far deeper is wrong (Romans 1:18).
A Universal Condition One of the most important things to notice in Romans is how Paul speaks about sin.
He does not treat sin merely as behavior.
He treats it as a condition that affects every person equally.
Romans unfolds a sweeping diagnosis:
Gentiles are accountable (Romans 1:18–32) Jews are accountable (Romans 2:1–24) The religious are accountable (Romans 2:17–29) The moral are accountable (Romans 2:1–3) No group is given an advantage when it comes to standing righteous before God (Romans 3:9).
This is not an argument against morality or religion —
it is an argument against using them as a foundation.
The Failure of Self-Assessment Human instinct leans toward comparison.
We measure ourselves:
against others against visible failures against our own intentions Romans removes the comfort of comparison by changing the standard.
The question is no longer:
“Am I better than someone else?”
The question becomes:
“Can I stand righteous before God?”
Once that question is asked honestly, comparison collapses (Romans 3:10–12).
Law Clarifies — It Does Not Cure One of Paul’s most misunderstood points in Romans is the role of law.
Law was never given to fix the problem it reveals.
Instead:
Law defines righteousness Law exposes failure Law removes excuses Paul states this plainly: through law comes the knowledge of sin (Romans 3:20).
This is why Romans insists that no one will be justified by law —
not because law is bad, but because the problem is deeper than instruction (Romans 3:19).
Telling a broken system how it should function does not repair it.
The Weight of the Diagnosis Romans presses the reader into an uncomfortable realization:
Even sincere effort cannot undo what already is.
This is why Romans does not move quickly to solution.
It wants the reader to feel the full weight of the problem (Romans 3:19).
Because if the problem is misunderstood, the solution will be reduced.
And if the solution is reduced, assurance will never be settled.
Why This Matters Many believers struggle not because they doubt God’s power,
but because they quietly assume that something is still required of them
before God’s verdict can be secure.
Romans dismantles that assumption at the root.
Until the problem is seen clearly:
grace feels risky assurance feels irresponsible rest feels premature But once the problem is understood, a surprising thing happens:
The solution no longer feels excessive — it feels necessary.
In the next chapter, Romans introduces the turning point —
not human resolve, but God’s action (Romans 3:21).
That is where everything changes.

body:
Chapter 3 God’s Solution, Not Ours Romans does not present God as responding to human effort.
It presents God as acting in response to human inability.
This distinction is critical.
If the solution depends on human action, then assurance will always remain fragile.
If the solution depends on God’s action, then assurance can finally rest.
Romans insists on the second (Romans 3:21).
A Righteousness Revealed At the center of Romans is a decisive shift.
After establishing the universal problem, Paul introduces something entirely new:
Not a new command,
not a refined law,
not a higher standard —
but a righteousness revealed (Romans 3:21–22).
This righteousness does not originate with humanity.
It does not emerge from obedience, sincerity, or moral progress.
It comes from God.
The Direction of the Action One of the most important observations in Romans is the direction of the action.
God is not reacting to human righteousness.
Human righteousness is responding to God’s action.
Paul is explicit:
God sets forth Christ (Romans 3:25) God justifies the one who believes (Romans 3:26) Humanity receives the outcome by faith (Romans 3:22) When this order is reversed, the message collapses.
Justification Is a Verdict Romans does not describe justification as a process.
It describes it as a verdict.
Paul uses courtroom language intentionally:
to justify is to declare righteous (Romans 3:24; Romans 4:5).
A verdict does not evolve.
It is declared.
And once declared by the proper authority, it stands.
Grace Removes Boasting If justification comes from God’s action alone, something must disappear.
Boasting.
Paul states this directly: boasting is excluded (Romans 3:27).
There is nothing left to claim:
no contribution no leverage no personal addition Grace does not leave room for partial credit.
This is not humiliating — it is liberating.
Faith as the Means, Not the Merit Faith plays a central role in Romans, but not the role many assume.
Faith is not presented as a virtue that earns God’s response.
It is presented as the means of receiving what God has done.
Paul contrasts faith with works explicitly (Romans 4:4–5).
Faith looks outward, not inward.
It rests in God’s action, not human strength.
Why This Changes Everything Once God’s solution is seen clearly, the weight lifts.
Fear loses its grip Performance loses its pressure Assurance gains its footing The believer is no longer trying to secure a future verdict.
They are learning to live from one already given (Romans 5:1).
Romans does not invite the reader to try harder.
It invites the reader to believe what God has already declared.
In the next chapter, Paul addresses the question that inevitably follows:
If justification is truly God’s verdict —
can it ever be undone?

body:
Chapter 4 It Is Settled At this point in Romans, a question naturally arises:
If justification is truly God’s verdict,
can it ever be undone?
Paul does not avoid that question.
He answers it by strengthening the claim.
Romans does not move toward uncertainty —
it moves toward assurance (Romans 5:1–2).
Peace Is the Result, Not the Goal Romans presents peace with God as something that follows justification, not something pursued afterward.
Paul states it simply:
having been justified by faith, we have peace with God (Romans 5:1).
Peace is not achieved through spiritual effort.
It is received as a consequence of what God has already declared.
When peace is treated as a goal, it becomes fragile.
When peace is received as a result, it becomes stable.
The Logic of Permanence Paul’s argument in Romans is not emotional.
It is logical.
If justification depends on God’s righteousness,
then its permanence depends on God’s integrity.
Paul grounds assurance not in the believer’s consistency,
but in God’s demonstrated action in Christ (Romans 5:6–8).
To suggest that justification can be reversed is to suggest that God’s verdict was premature or incomplete.
Romans allows neither option.
God does not issue provisional declarations.
Where Assurance Actually Comes From Assurance is often treated as a feeling.
Romans treats it as a conclusion.
It does not arise from introspection,
but from understanding what God has done.
This is why Paul repeatedly points the reader outward —
to Christ’s work, not the believer’s performance (Romans 5:9–10).
The clearer the verdict, the stronger the assurance.
Why Doubt Persists Even with clear teaching, many believers still struggle with doubt.
Romans exposes why:
Doubt survives when justification is quietly re-defined Fear remains when peace is treated as conditional Anxiety grows when the focus returns to self Paul warns that shifting foundations always weaken confidence (Romans 4:20–21).
Assurance falters whenever trust moves away from God’s promise and back onto human stability.
Settled Means Settled The phrase “it is settled” is not meant to be poetic.
It is meant to be precise.
What God has declared righteous, He does not later place under review (Romans 8:33).
What God has reconciled, He does not later distance (Romans 5:10).
What God has justified, He does not later threaten (Romans 8:1).
This is not optimism.
It is consistency.
Living Under a Final Verdict When the verdict is understood as final, something changes.
The believer no longer lives under evaluation,
but under acceptance (Romans 5:2).
This does not lead to indifference.
It leads to freedom.
And freedom, Romans will show, produces a very different kind of life.
In the final chapter, we turn to the practical question:
If the matter is settled —
how should that reality shape the way we live?

body:
Chapter 5 Living From What Is Settled Once the verdict is understood as final, a new question emerges:
If everything is settled,
what motivation remains for the Christian life?
Romans does not treat this as a problem.
It treats it as the inevitable next step (Romans 6:1).
A Common Fear Many assume that assurance will weaken devotion.
If acceptance is secure,
won’t obedience become optional?
Won’t seriousness fade?
Won’t effort disappear?
Paul anticipates this concern and addresses it directly, not by retreating from assurance, but by clarifying its purpose (Romans 6:2).
Motivation Changes When Fear Is Removed Fear is a powerful motivator, but it is a poor foundation.
Fear can produce:
conformity restraint short-term compliance But fear cannot produce life.
Romans shows that when fear is removed, something better takes its place:
gratitude.
Paul contrasts life under obligation with life under grace (Romans 6:14).
Obedience that flows from fear asks:
“What must I do to avoid loss?”
Obedience that flows from assurance asks:
“How can I respond to what I have received?” (Romans 12:1)
Identity Precedes Behavior One of the most consistent patterns in Romans is order.
Paul never tells the believer who to become
before telling them who they are (Romans 6:6–7).
The Christian life is not about striving toward identity.
It is about living in alignment with an identity already given (Romans 8:1).
When behavior is separated from identity, it becomes exhausting.
When behavior flows from identity, it becomes natural.
Grace Does Not Compete With Transformation A common assumption is that grace and growth are opposites.
Romans refuses that framing.
Grace is not the enemy of transformation —
it is the only environment where transformation can actually occur.
Paul explains that freedom from condemnation creates the space in which new life operates (Romans 8:2–4).
Change driven by pressure produces performance.
Change driven by grace produces renewal.
This is why Romans moves from justification to life,
not back to evaluation (Romans 8:10–11).
Freedom Produces Fruit Romans does not call believers to live recklessly.
It calls them to live freely.
And freedom, when properly understood, produces fruit.
Paul describes this shift clearly: having been set free, believers now bear fruit leading to life (Romans 6:22).
The believer who is no longer trying to secure God’s favor
is finally able to live without self-obsession.
That freedom creates space for love, service, and endurance.
A Different Way to Read Scripture Living from what is settled also changes how Scripture is read.
Commands are no longer threats.
Warnings are no longer predictions of loss.
Exhortations are no longer measurements of worth.
They become guidance —
spoken to those who are already secure (Romans 15:4).
This shift does not weaken Scripture.
It clarifies it.
The Rest That Remains Romans ends not with anxiety,
but with confidence in God’s faithfulness.
Paul’s final note is certainty: nothing can separate the believer from the love of God in Christ (Romans 8:38–39).
The final posture of the believer is not vigilance,
but rest.
Not passive rest —
but settled rest.
Rest that knows the verdict is final.
Rest that trusts the One who issued it.
Final Thought The message of Romans is not complicated.
It is demanding —
because it removes our ability to contribute (Romans 11:36).
But once received, it simplifies everything.
What God has settled,
does not need to be defended, improved, or maintained.
It only needs to be believed.
And from that belief,
life follows.

body:
It Is Settled
From God’s Perspective
John Wry
Chapter 1 Reality Begins With God Reality does not begin with human questions. It begins with God making Himself known. Scripture exists because God is not silent. He reveals who He is, how He relates to humanity, and what He has done. The story of the gospel is not the story of people discovering God, but of God disclosing reality before it was understood.
This matters because revelation establishes objectivity. Truth is not constructed from experience, refined through reflection, or reached through effort. It is revealed. God speaks before we respond. He acts before we interpret. Sin explains why revelation is necessary, but it does not initiate the story. God does.
When this order is reversed, confusion follows. When revelation is replaced with interpretation, everything loses its weight. Grace can sound like opinion. Truth can sound like perspective. Assurance can sound like confidence. Scripture can sound like advice.
None of those things are false—but none of them are stable.
Romans refuses to build on instability. It does not begin by asking how people should live. It begins by declaring what is true about God, about humanity, and about the situation between them. Only after reality is disclosed does life begin to make sense.
This is why Scripture consistently moves from revelation to response. God reveals Himself, then calls for understanding. God acts, then explains the meaning of that action. God speaks, then invites trust. The order is never reversed. Life is not the starting point; it is the outcome.
When God is not clearly recognized as the one who defines reality, people instinctively look elsewhere for grounding. Experience begins to interpret truth. Feelings begin to weigh more than declaration. Sincerity begins to substitute for certainty. The result is not rebellion, but instability.
This instability explains why many sincere people feel unsettled even while believing the right things. They may affirm grace, but experience still feels authoritative. They may agree with truth, but perspective still feels negotiable. They may believe Scripture, but hear it as guidance rather than disclosure.
The issue was never grace — it was authority.
More precisely, it was the question of who has the right to define what is true.
Scripture’s answer is unambiguous. God defines reality because God reveals Himself. The gospel does not begin with human need, human effort, or human failure. It begins with God acting, God speaking, and God making Himself known.
Only when this is settled can anything else be understood rightly.
This chapter does not explain the human problem yet. It establishes the reference point. Before Scripture diagnoses sin, it reveals God. Before it addresses collapse, it establishes truth. Before it speaks about how to live, it declares what is real.
Everything that follows depends on this.
It is settled from God’s perspective. Reality does not wait for our agreement to become true.
Reflection Questions · What do I instinctively treat as the starting point for reality—God’s revelation or my experience?
· Have I been hearing Scripture more as advice than as disclosure?
· Where might truth have felt negotiable because God was no longer the reference point?
· What changes if reality is something revealed rather than something interpreted?
· What would it mean for life to proceed from what God has declared rather than toward it?
Chapter 2 God Is Displaced When God reveals Himself, a response is unavoidable. Revelation does not force obedience, but it does remove neutrality. Once God is known, reality is no longer undefined. The question is no longer whether truth exists, but whether God will remain the reference point for it.
Romans describes what happens when He does not.
This is not the story of people rejecting rules or resisting authority in general. It is the story of exchange. God is not denied; He is displaced. Something else quietly takes His place as the starting point for meaning, judgment, and direction. Truth does not disappear—it is relocated.
When God is displaced, truth is no longer anchored in who He is. It becomes tethered to something closer, more manageable, and more comfortable. Human experience begins to interpret reality. Desire begins to shape value. Preference begins to guide judgment. What was once received is now rearranged.
This shift is subtle, but its effects are catastrophic.
The First Collapse: Human Conduct Romans describes the initial consequence of this displacement in the most visible way—human conduct. Actions become disordered, not merely immoral. Behavior no longer flows from what is true about God, but from what now feels reasonable without Him as the reference point.
This is not ignorance. God has been made known. The issue is not lack of information, but loss of orientation. When truth is no longer anchored in God, behavior begins to drift. What once restrained no longer carries weight. What once warned now feels excessive.
This is the first collapse. But it is not the deepest.
The Second Collapse: Human Values As conduct drifts, values begin to invert. What people approve, celebrate, excuse, or defend shifts accordingly. Romans describes this not as confusion, but as exchange—a trading of what is fitting for what is convenient.
At this stage, sin is no longer merely practiced; it is justified. What once felt wrong now feels normal. What once provoked discomfort now provokes defense. The problem is no longer simply what people do, but what they consider acceptable.
This is not moral weakness. It is moral reorientation.
What restrains behavior no longer restrains because values themselves have changed.
The Final Collapse: Human Thinking The deepest collapse Romans describes is not behavioral or ethical—it is cognitive. Human thinking itself becomes unreliable as an authority. Reason no longer moves toward truth, but away from it. Logic is not lost; it is repurposed.
At this point, people are not merely doing what is wrong or approving what is wrong. They are thinking from the wrong starting point. Reality is reinterpreted to justify what has already been chosen. Truth is no longer discovered; it is defended selectively.
This is why Romans describes the mind itself as affected. When God is displaced, thinking does not remain neutral. It becomes captive to whatever has replaced Him.
This is the final collapse. And it explains everything that came before it.
Why the Situation Is Devastating This progression matters because it removes every false hope of self-recovery. If conduct is disordered, values are inverted, and thinking is corrupted, then humanity cannot correct itself by trying harder, knowing more, or organizing better.
The situation is not improving. It is not manageable. It is devastating.
This is not because people are especially evil. It is because the reference point has been lost. When God no longer defines reality, nothing else can hold the weight.
This is why Scripture must speak. This is why revelation is necessary. This is why the gospel cannot begin with instruction or reform. There is nothing stable left to build on.
Romans does not describe this to shame humanity. It describes it to establish the legitimacy of what God will do next.
Reflection Questions · Where do I see truth quietly being interpreted through experience rather than revealed by God?
· Have I assumed the problem was behavior when the issue was orientation?
· In what ways have values shifted to support what is already practiced?
· Do I trust my thinking as neutral, or do I recognize the need for revelation?
· Why would recovery require God to act rather than humanity to improve?
Chapter 3 God Acts If Chapter 1 established the reference point and Chapter 2 established the collapse, Chapter 3 introduces the only possible response.
God acts.
This is not a reaction to human effort, improvement, or repentance. It is not triggered by moral recovery or spiritual insight. Romans presents God’s action as necessary precisely because humanity cannot act its way out of the situation it is in.
When God is displaced, truth is misplaced. When truth is misplaced, thinking collapses. When thinking collapses, recovery from within is impossible.
At that point, action must come from outside the system.
The Action Does Not Originate With Humanity Romans is careful here. God’s action is not described as assistance, partnership, or reinforcement. It is not God helping humanity do what it was already trying to do. It is God doing what humanity could not do at all.
This is why the gospel is not introduced as advice or opportunity. It is introduced as intervention.
God does not wait for clarity. God does not wait for readiness. God does not wait for improvement.
He acts because reality requires it.
Righteousness Is Established, Not Requested When God acts, He does not merely forgive. He establishes righteousness. This matters because forgiveness alone would leave the underlying question unresolved. Forgiveness without righteousness would still place reality on unstable ground.
Romans insists on something stronger.
God’s action is righteous because it is consistent with who He is. It does not overlook sin, minimize collapse, or pretend devastation was exaggerated. It addresses the problem at its deepest level.
The issue was not that humanity needed encouragement. The issue was that humanity stood exposed before truth.
God’s action answers that exposure definitively.
The Resolution Is Objective This is where Romans quietly changes everything.
God’s action does not depend on human awareness, agreement, or experience. It establishes a reality that exists whether it is felt or not. Resolution is not measured by peace of mind but by divine declaration.
This is why Romans speaks in the language of verdict and standing. Something is said to be true before anything is felt to be different. Reality changes first. Experience follows later.
This does not dismiss experience. It explains it.
Lingering guilt does not undo the verdict. Ongoing struggle does not reopen the case. Delayed clarity does not negate what has been established.
God’s action stands independent of all of it.
Reflection Questions · Do I instinctively think of God’s action as help, or as intervention?
· Have I treated forgiveness as sufficient without considering righteousness?
· What fears arise when resolution is declared before experience changes?
· Why would God’s action need to stand independent of how I feel about it?
· What becomes possible if reality has already changed?
Chapter 4 Standing Is Established Once God acts, a new question emerges—not about effort, but about position.
Where does this leave us?
Romans does not rush past this question. It slows down to make something unmistakably clear: God’s action establishes standing before it addresses experience. Before life is described, position is secured. Before anything changes outwardly, something is settled inwardly—but not subjectively. Objectively.
Standing is not a feeling. It is a fact.
Standing Is Not Earned, Maintained, or Improved Romans is careful to remove every familiar category. Standing before God is not achieved through effort, protected through consistency, or strengthened through time. It is not fragile. It is not provisional. It does not fluctuate with awareness or performance.
Standing is established because God has acted.
This matters because many people assume standing must be kept. Even after hearing about grace, they quietly believe acceptance is conditional, peace is delicate, and access can be interrupted. Life becomes about maintenance. Faith becomes vigilance.
Romans refuses that framework.
Standing is described as something already in place—not something we are trying to secure.
Peace Is Declared Before It Is Felt One of the first ways Romans describes this standing is peace. Not peace as emotion, but peace as condition. Peace does not begin in the heart; it begins in the situation. The conflict that mattered most has been resolved.
This is why peace can be declared even when anxiety remains.
Peace is not the absence of struggle. It is the absence of hostility. It means the relationship itself is no longer in question. Whatever turmoil remains, it no longer defines the core reality.
This is difficult for many to accept, because we often treat peace as a sensation rather than a status. But Romans insists that peace is something that exists whether it is felt or not.
That distinction explains much confusion.
Access Is Granted, Not Achieved Alongside peace, Romans speaks of access. This language is deliberate. Access implies permission. It assumes the question of approach has already been answered.
Access is not something we create by confidence or sustain through performance. It is granted because standing has been established. We do not move closer by behaving better. We come freely because we already belong there.
This is not casual language. It describes a new position—a place we now occupy because God has acted.
Standing determines access, not the other way around.
Reflection Questions · Do I treat standing as something settled or something I must protect?
· Have I confused peace of situation with peace of feeling?
· Do I approach God as one with access or one seeking permission?
· Where have fear and suspicion reinterpreted what God has declared?
· What would change if life truly proceeded from established standing?
Chapter 5 Sin Repositioned Once standing is established, a question immediately presses itself forward.
If the situation between God and humanity has been resolved, what place does sin now occupy? If condemnation no longer defines the relationship, does sin still matter? And if it does, how can it be addressed without undoing what has already been settled?
Romans does not avoid this tension. It anticipates it. The objection is not new, careless, or rebellious. It is logical. And it must be answered carefully.
Sin is not dismissed. But it is no longer allowed to sit where it once did.
Sin Is No Longer the Defining Issue Before God acted, sin defined the situation. It exposed humanity, fractured relationship, and demanded judgment. But once God acts decisively, sin no longer occupies that role. It does not disappear, but it is displaced.
This distinction matters.
Sin is still present, still destructive, still capable of harm. But it is no longer the lens through which everything else is interpreted. It no longer defines standing. It no longer governs access. It no longer determines belonging.
Romans insists on this shift. If sin were allowed to remain the defining issue, standing would collapse back into uncertainty and life would revert to maintenance.
That is precisely what Romans refuses to allow.
Reflection Questions · Do I instinctively treat sin as the defining issue after resolution?
· Have I organized my life around avoiding sin rather than understanding standing?
· Where has fear quietly replaced clarity as a motivator?
· How might sin lose power if it no longer interprets my relationship with God?
· What changes when sin is taken seriously but not given authority?
Chapter 6 Struggle Explained Once sin is repositioned, a new tension emerges—one that many people quietly fear.
If standing is settled, if sin no longer defines the relationship, then why does struggle remain? Why does desire conflict with intention? Why does clarity not immediately produce consistency?
Romans does not treat this tension as failure. It treats it as expected.
Struggle does not mean the resolution was incomplete. It means life is now being lived in a new reality that is still being learned.
Reflection Questions · Do I interpret struggle as evidence against what God has settled?
· Where have I allowed conflict to redefine my standing?
· Have I mistaken awareness for failure?
· What changes if struggle is understood as transition rather than threat?
· How might clarity reshape the way I respond to inner conflict?
Chapter 7 Life From a Settled Place Only now does Romans begin to speak meaningfully about life.
Not because life was unimportant before, but because life cannot be understood until standing is settled, sin is repositioned, and struggle is explained. Any attempt to describe life earlier would have turned clarity into pressure and truth into demand.
Romans refuses to do that.
Life is not presented as a way to secure what God has given. It is presented as the expression of what has already been established.
Reflection Questions · Have I been trying to produce outcomes without sharing the source?
· Where has effort been driven by fear rather than clarity?
· What changes if life is learned rather than forced?
· How does standing reshape motivation?
· What does alignment look like when acceptance is no longer at stake?
Chapter 8 Nothing Reopens the Case Romans does not end by urging confidence. It ends by revealing why confidence is unavoidable.
Assurance is not introduced as a spiritual virtue or emotional posture. It is presented as the logical outcome of everything that has already been established.
Assurance is not confidence in our perspective of God. It is alignment with God’s perspective of us. The case is settled—not because we feel secure, but because God has spoken.
Reflection Questions · Where have I treated assurance as risky rather than required?
· Have I confused humility with uncertainty?
· What fears depend on the verdict being reversible?
· How does suffering look different when separation is no longer possible?
· What does it mean to live under a reality that cannot be reopened?
Chapter 9 Israel: History as Proof At this point in the argument, a serious question cannot be avoided.
If God’s verdict is settled, if nothing can separate from His love, if His purpose cannot be undone—what about Israel?
Israel’s story is not theoretical. It is historical, public, and unresolved in human terms. If God’s faithfulness can fail there, then everything said about assurance becomes fragile. Romans does not avoid this tension. It confronts it directly.
Paul turns to Israel not to revise his claims, but to prove them.
Israel is not an illustration—it is evidence.
Romans does not use Israel as a metaphor or object lesson. Israel is presented as a real people, with real promises, real failure, real judgment, and real continuity. Their history is not clean. It is marked by rebellion, exile, discipline, and apparent abandonment.
And yet, God does not erase them.
If God’s purposes were dependent on human consistency, Israel would be the clearest counterexample. If faithfulness could be nullified by disobedience, the story would have ended long ago. But Romans insists that it has not.
Israel stands as living proof that God does not reverse Himself.
Failure does not cancel calling.
Romans is explicit: Israel’s failure is real, but it is not final. Discipline occurs, but rejection does not. Hardening is described, but abandonment is denied. The calling remains intact even when response falters.
No replacement, no revision.
Romans is careful here. Israel is not replaced. The promises are not reassigned. God does not save face by redefining what He meant. The faithfulness on display is not abstract—it is specific, stubborn, and historically traceable.
History confirms the verdict.
By the time Romans finishes this section, the reader is meant to see something clearly: assurance is not a fragile doctrine built on ideal cases. It is grounded in God’s demonstrated faithfulness across time, failure, discipline, and delay.
Israel is the proof that assurance is justified.
Reflection Questions · Why does Israel’s history strengthen assurance rather than weaken it?
· How does God’s faithfulness through failure protect confidence today?
· What does discipline look like when it does not mean abandonment?
Concluding Chapter Living Under a Settled Reality This book has not tried to tell you how to live. It has tried to clarify what is true.
Everything that follows in life depends on that order. When reality is unclear, life becomes anxious. When standing is uncertain, effort becomes exhausting. When truth feels negotiable, fear quietly takes control.
Reality begins with God. God makes Himself known.
That restoration does not begin with behavior. It begins with resolution.
The Christian life does not move toward acceptance. It moves from it.
The gospel does not ask you to complete what God has begun. It invites you to live in what God has already settled.
It is settled—not because we have resolved it, but because God has.
Final Reflection · What changes when reality is something received rather than achieved?
· Where has life been driven by uncertainty instead of clarity?
· How does effort look different when it no longer carries the weight of standing?
· What fears lose their power if the verdict truly stands?
· What does it mean to live under a reality that cannot be reopened?

From God’s Perspective
John Wry

body:
Está Resuelto Desde la perspectiva de Dios Un recorrido claro y sencillo por el mensaje de Romanos Autor: John Wry
Propósito de este libro Este libro fue escrito para ayudar al lector a comprender una verdad simple, pero a menudo malentendida:
Lo que Dios ha resuelto, no necesita ser re-resuelto por nosotros.
Muchos creyentes viven como si su posición delante de Dios fuera frágil, incierta o dependiente de su desempeño continuo. El mensaje de Romanos presenta un panorama muy distinto: uno en el que Dios actúa de manera decisiva, justa y final.
Este libro no intenta ser técnico ni académico.
Está escrito con un lenguaje claro y reflexivo, para que el lector pueda seguir el argumento de Pablo sin necesidad de formación teológica previa.
Romanos será tratado como un mensaje que debe ser entendido, no solo como versículos que deben ser citados.
Cómo usar este libro Lea con calma Siga el argumento tal como se desarrolla Permita que las conclusiones surjan del texto Resista la tentación de insertar suposiciones El objetivo no es decirle qué pensar, sino ayudarle a ver qué está diciendo realmente el texto.
Introducción Por qué “Está Resuelto” Pocas preguntas generan tanta tensión en la vida cristiana como esta:
¿Estoy realmente seguro delante de Dios?
Para muchos, la seguridad parece temporal. Sube y baja según la obediencia, las emociones, las disciplinas espirituales o los fracasos recientes. Incluso creyentes sinceros viven con el temor silencioso de que algo todavía pueda salir mal.
La carta a los Romanos confronta directamente ese temor.
Pablo no construye su argumento sobre la fidelidad humana, sino sobre la acción de Dios, la justicia de Dios y la integridad de Dios (Romanos 1:16–17). Desde los primeros capítulos, Romanos conduce al lector hacia una sola conclusión:
Lo que Dios ha declarado justo, no lo vuelve a poner en duda.
Una lucha común Muchos cristianos sostienen dos ideas al mismo tiempo:
Dios justifica gratuitamente al pecador (Romanos 3:24). El creyente debe mantener esa justificación mediante su esfuerzo. Romanos no permite que estas dos ideas convivan cómodamente.
Si la justicia es acreditada sin obras, entonces no es sostenida por obras (Romanos 4:4–5).
Si la paz con Dios es establecida por la acción de Dios, no puede ser deshecha por la debilidad humana (Romanos 5:1).
Esta tensión no es teórica. Afecta la manera en que las personas oran, sirven, obedecen e incluso leen la Biblia.
Por qué Romanos es importante Romanos no fue escrito para enseñar a los creyentes cómo volverse espirituales.
Fue escrito para explicar lo que Dios ya ha hecho.
El argumento de Pablo se desarrolla con cuidado:
El problema universal del pecado (Romanos 1:18–3:20) La incapacidad de la ley para resolverlo (Romanos 3:19–20) La acción decisiva de Dios en Cristo (Romanos 3:21–26) La certeza de la justificación (Romanos 4:1–8) Las implicaciones de esa certeza para la vida (Romanos 5–8) Este libro sigue ese mismo flujo, permitiendo que Romanos hable en sus propios términos.
Lo que este libro es — y no es Este libro es:
Una explicación de Romanos en lenguaje sencillo Enfocado en claridad y coherencia Arraigado en el texto bíblico Este libro no es:
Un debate teológico Un sistema doctrinal Un manual de desempeño cristiano El objetivo es la comprensión, porque la claridad produce descanso, y el descanso produce fruto (Romanos 6:22).
En el próximo capítulo comenzamos donde comienza Pablo:
no con soluciones, sino con el problema que hace necesaria la solución (Romanos 1:18).
Capítulo 1 El problema que Romanos expone Antes de que Romanos ofrezca seguridad, exige claridad.
Pablo no comienza con consuelo.
Comienza con verdad — y la verdad no es halagadora.
Romanos no intenta convencer al lector de que necesita mejorar.
Insiste en que hay algo mucho más profundo que está mal (Romanos 1:18).
Una condición universal Una de las observaciones más importantes en Romanos es cómo Pablo habla del pecado.
No lo trata simplemente como conducta.
Lo trata como una condición que afecta a todos por igual.
Romanos desarrolla un diagnóstico amplio:
Los gentiles son responsables (Romanos 1:18–32) Los judíos son responsables (Romanos 2:1–24) Los religiosos son responsables (Romanos 2:17–29) Los morales son responsables (Romanos 2:1–3) Nadie tiene ventaja cuando se trata de estar justo delante de Dios (Romanos 3:9).
Esto no es un argumento contra la moralidad o la religión,
sino contra usarlas como fundamento.
El fracaso de la autoevaluación El instinto humano se inclina a la comparación.
Nos medimos:
con otros con fracasos visibles con nuestras propias intenciones Romanos elimina la comodidad de la comparación al cambiar el estándar.
La pregunta ya no es:
“¿Soy mejor que otros?”
La pregunta pasa a ser:
“¿Puedo presentarme justo delante de Dios?”
Cuando esa pregunta se enfrenta con honestidad, la comparación se derrumba (Romanos 3:10–12).
La ley aclara — no cura Uno de los puntos más malentendidos en Romanos es el propósito de la ley.
La ley nunca fue dada para solucionar el problema que revela.
Más bien:
la ley define la justicia la ley expone el fracaso la ley elimina excusas Pablo lo dice claramente: por medio de la ley viene el conocimiento del pecado (Romanos 3:20).
Por eso Romanos afirma que nadie será justificado por la ley —
no porque la ley sea mala, sino porque el problema es más profundo que la instrucción (Romanos 3:19).
Decirle a un sistema roto cómo debería funcionar no lo repara.
El peso del diagnóstico Romanos lleva al lector a una conclusión incómoda:
Ni siquiera el esfuerzo sincero puede deshacer lo que ya es.
Por eso Romanos no se apresura a presentar la solución.
Quiere que el lector sienta el peso completo del problema (Romanos 3:19).
Porque si el problema se entiende mal, la solución se reducirá.
Y si la solución se reduce, la seguridad nunca quedará resuelta.
Por qué esto importa Muchos creyentes luchan no porque duden del poder de Dios,
sino porque asumen silenciosamente que algo todavía se les exige
antes de que el veredicto de Dios sea seguro.
Romanos desmantela esa suposición desde la raíz.
Hasta que el problema se ve con claridad:
la gracia parece arriesgada la seguridad parece irresponsable el descanso parece prematuro Pero cuando el problema se entiende, sucede algo sorprendente:
La solución ya no parece excesiva — parece necesaria.
En el próximo capítulo, Romanos presenta el punto de giro:
no la resolución humana, sino la acción de Dios (Romanos 3:21).
Capítulo 2 La solución de Dios, no la nuestra Romanos no presenta a Dios reaccionando al esfuerzo humano.
Lo presenta actuando frente a la incapacidad humana.
Esta distinción es crucial.
Si la solución depende de la acción humana, la seguridad siempre será frágil.
Si depende de la acción de Dios, la seguridad finalmente puede descansar.
Romanos insiste en lo segundo (Romanos 3:21).
Una justicia revelada En el centro de Romanos hay un giro decisivo.
Después de establecer el problema universal, Pablo introduce algo completamente nuevo:
No un nuevo mandato,
no una ley refinada,
no un estándar más alto —
sino una justicia revelada (Romanos 3:21–22).
Esta justicia no se origina en el ser humano.
No surge de la obediencia, la sinceridad ni el progreso moral.
Proviene de Dios.
La dirección de la acción Una de las observaciones más importantes en Romanos es la dirección de la acción.
Dios no responde a la justicia humana.
La justicia humana responde a la acción de Dios.
Pablo es explícito:
Dios presenta a Cristo (Romanos 3:25) Dios justifica al que cree (Romanos 3:26) El ser humano recibe el resultado por medio de la fe (Romanos 3:22) Cuando este orden se invierte, el mensaje colapsa.
La justificación es un veredicto Romanos no describe la justificación como un proceso.
La describe como un veredicto.
Pablo usa lenguaje judicial de forma intencional:
justificar es declarar justo (Romanos 3:24; Romanos 4:5).
Un veredicto no evoluciona.
Se pronuncia.
Y una vez pronunciado por la autoridad correcta, permanece.
La gracia elimina la jactancia Si la justificación proviene únicamente de la acción de Dios, algo debe desaparecer.
La jactancia.
Pablo lo afirma directamente: la jactancia queda excluida (Romanos 3:27).
No queda nada que reclamar:
ninguna contribución ninguna ventaja ninguna adición personal La gracia no deja espacio para el crédito parcial.
Esto no es humillante — es liberador.
La fe como medio, no como mérito La fe ocupa un lugar central en Romanos, pero no el que muchos suponen.
La fe no se presenta como una virtud que provoca la respuesta de Dios.
Se presenta como el medio para recibir lo que Dios ha hecho.
Pablo contrasta explícitamente la fe con las obras (Romanos 4:4–5).
La fe mira hacia afuera, no hacia adentro.
Descansa en la acción de Dios, no en la fuerza humana.
Por qué esto lo cambia todo Cuando la solución de Dios se ve con claridad, el peso se levanta.
El miedo pierde su control El desempeño pierde su presión La seguridad encuentra su base El creyente ya no intenta asegurar un veredicto futuro.
Aprende a vivir desde uno ya pronunciado (Romanos 5:1).
Romanos no invita al lector a esforzarse más.
Lo invita a creer lo que Dios ya ha declarado.
En el próximo capítulo, Pablo aborda la pregunta inevitable:
Si la justificación es realmente el veredicto de Dios,
¿puede alguna vez ser deshecha?
Capítulo 3 Está resuelto En este punto de Romanos surge una pregunta natural:
Si la justificación es verdaderamente el veredicto de Dios,
¿puede ser revertida?
Pablo no evita esa pregunta.
La responde reforzando la afirmación.
Romanos no avanza hacia la incertidumbre,
sino hacia la seguridad (Romanos 5:1–2).
La paz es el resultado, no la meta Romanos presenta la paz con Dios como algo que sigue a la justificación, no como algo que se busca después.
Pablo lo dice con claridad:
habiendo sido justificados por la fe, tenemos paz para con Dios (Romanos 5:1).
La paz no se logra mediante esfuerzo espiritual.
Se recibe como consecuencia de lo que Dios ya ha declarado.
Cuando la paz se trata como meta, se vuelve frágil.
Cuando se recibe como resultado, se vuelve estable.
La lógica de la permanencia El argumento de Pablo en Romanos no es emocional.
Es lógico.
Si la justificación depende de la justicia de Dios,
entonces su permanencia depende de la integridad de Dios.
Pablo fundamenta la seguridad no en la constancia del creyente,
sino en la acción demostrada de Dios en Cristo (Romanos 5:6–8).
Sugerir que la justificación puede revertirse es sugerir que el veredicto de Dios fue prematuro o incompleto.
Romanos no permite ninguna de las dos opciones.
Dios no emite declaraciones provisionales.
De dónde proviene realmente la seguridad La seguridad suele tratarse como una emoción.
Romanos la trata como una conclusión.
No surge de la introspección,
sino de comprender lo que Dios ha hecho.
Por eso Pablo dirige repetidamente la atención hacia afuera,
hacia la obra de Cristo, no hacia el desempeño del creyente (Romanos 5:9–10).
Cuanto más claro es el veredicto, más firme es la seguridad.
Por qué persiste la duda Incluso con enseñanza clara, muchos creyentes siguen luchando con la duda.
Romanos expone por qué:
La duda persiste cuando la justificación se redefine silenciosamente El temor permanece cuando la paz se trata como condicional La ansiedad crece cuando el enfoque vuelve al yo Pablo advierte que cambiar el fundamento siempre debilita la confianza (Romanos 4:20–21).
La seguridad se debilita cada vez que la confianza se desplaza de la promesa de Dios a la estabilidad humana.
Capítulo 4 Vivir bajo un veredicto final La frase “está resuelto” no pretende ser poética.
Pretende ser precisa.
Lo que Dios ha declarado justo, no lo vuelve a poner en revisión (Romanos 8:33).
Lo que Dios ha reconciliado, no lo vuelve a distanciar (Romanos 5:10).
Lo que Dios ha justificado, no lo vuelve a condenar (Romanos 8:1).
Cuando el veredicto se entiende como final, algo cambia:
El creyente ya no vive bajo evaluación,
sino bajo aceptación (Romanos 5:2).
Esto no conduce a la indiferencia.
Conduce a la libertad.
Y la libertad, como mostrará Romanos, produce un tipo de vida muy distinto.
Capítulo 5 Vivir desde lo que está resuelto Cuando el veredicto se comprende como final, surge una nueva pregunta:
Si todo está resuelto,
¿qué motivación queda para la vida cristiana?
Romanos no trata esto como un problema.
Lo trata como el siguiente paso inevitable (Romanos 6:1).
Un temor común Muchos suponen que la seguridad debilita la devoción.
Si la aceptación es segura,
¿no se volverá opcional la obediencia?
¿No se perderá la seriedad?
¿No desaparecerá el esfuerzo?
Pablo anticipa esta preocupación y la enfrenta directamente,
no retrocediendo en la seguridad,
sino aclarando su propósito (Romanos 6:2).
La motivación cambia cuando se elimina el temor El temor es un motivador poderoso, pero es un mal fundamento.
El temor puede producir:
conformidad contención cumplimiento a corto plazo Pero no puede producir vida.
Romanos muestra que cuando el temor se elimina, algo mejor ocupa su lugar:
la gratitud.
Pablo contrasta la vida bajo obligación con la vida bajo gracia (Romanos 6:14).
La obediencia que nace del temor pregunta:
“¿Qué debo hacer para evitar perder?”
La obediencia que nace de la seguridad pregunta:
“¿Cómo puedo responder a lo que he recibido?” (Romanos 12:1)
La identidad precede a la conducta Uno de los patrones más consistentes en Romanos es el orden.
Pablo nunca le dice al creyente quién debe llegar a ser
antes de decirle quién ya es (Romanos 6:6–7).
La vida cristiana no consiste en esforzarse para alcanzar una identidad.
Consiste en vivir en coherencia con una identidad ya otorgada (Romanos 8:1).
Cuando la conducta se separa de la identidad, se vuelve agotadora.
Cuando la conducta fluye de la identidad, se vuelve natural.
La gracia no compite con la transformación Una suposición común es que la gracia y el crecimiento se oponen.
Romanos rechaza ese marco.
La gracia no es enemiga de la transformación —
es el único entorno donde la transformación realmente puede ocurrir.
Pablo explica que la libertad de la condenación crea el espacio donde opera la vida nueva (Romanos 8:2–4).
El cambio impulsado por la presión produce desempeño.
El cambio impulsado por la gracia produce renovación.
Por eso Romanos avanza de la justificación a la vida,
no de regreso a la evaluación (Romanos 8:10–11).
La libertad produce fruto Romanos no llama a los creyentes a vivir sin cuidado.
Los llama a vivir en libertad.
Y la libertad, cuando se entiende correctamente, produce fruto.
Pablo describe este cambio con claridad:
habiendo sido libertados, ahora producimos fruto que conduce a vida (Romanos 6:22).
El creyente que ya no intenta asegurar el favor de Dios
finalmente puede vivir sin obsesión consigo mismo.
Esa libertad crea espacio para el amor, el servicio y la perseverancia.
Una manera distinta de leer la Escritura Vivir desde lo que está resuelto también transforma la manera de leer la Biblia.
Los mandamientos ya no son amenazas.
Las advertencias ya no son predicciones de pérdida.
Las exhortaciones ya no son medidas de valor personal.
Se convierten en guía,
dirigida a quienes ya están seguros (Romanos 15:4).
Este cambio no debilita la Escritura.
La aclara.
El descanso que permanece Romanos no termina con ansiedad,
sino con confianza en la fidelidad de Dios.
La nota final de Pablo es certeza:
nada puede separar al creyente del amor de Dios en Cristo (Romanos 8:38–39).
La postura final del creyente no es vigilancia,
sino descanso.
No un descanso pasivo,
sino un descanso resuelto.
Un descanso que sabe que el veredicto es final.
Un descanso que confía en Aquel que lo pronunció.
Reflexión final El mensaje de Romanos no es complicado.
Es exigente,
porque elimina nuestra capacidad de contribuir (Romanos 11:36).
Pero una vez recibido, lo simplifica todo.
Lo que Dios ha resuelto
no necesita ser defendido, mejorado ni mantenido.
Solo necesita ser creído.
Y de esa fe,
la vida fluye.

1 John workflow 1 John 1 John 4:7-5:21 GOD IS LOVE Below is a macro-level, text-bound overview of Cycles 11–15 in 1 John, continuing the final major section anchored by the predicate “God is love” (1 John 4:8, 1 John 4:16). This stays observational, follows the Greek-driven flow, and treats each cycle as a diagnostic unit (source → manifestation → outcome), without importing theology or synthesis.
Macro Context (Cycles 11–15) Location: 1 John 4:7 – 5:21 Governing predicate: ὁ θεὸς ἀγάπη ἐστίν (“God is love”) Function of the section: To identify divine source and verify it through manifested love, confession, and life, in contrast to fear, hatred, and false testimony. Cycle 11 — Love as Evidence of Divine Origin 1 John 4:7–8
Textual Focus
Loving one another (ἀγαπῶμεν ἀλλήλους – “let us love one another”) Source language: ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ (“from God”) Macro Observation
Love is presented as derivative, not autonomous. Two parallel claims: The one loving has been begotten of God and knows God The one not loving does not know God The cycle grounds the entire section by tying love directly to divine origin, not behavioral effort. Diagnostic Function
Love functions as evidence of source, not as a command under threat. Cycle 12 — Love Defined by Divine Action 1 John 4:9–10
Textual Focus
Manifestation language: ἐφανερώθη (“was manifested”) Historical action: God sending the Son Macro Observation
Love is defined, not assumed. The direction of love is God → us, not reciprocal. Two clarifications: Love is revealed in sending Love is clarified as not originating in human love Diagnostic Function
This cycle anchors love objectively in God’s action, preventing subjective redefinition. Cycle 13 — Love Completed in Mutual Indwelling 1 John 4:11–16
Textual Focus
Indwelling language: μένειν (“to remain / abide”) Confession: Ἰησοῦς ἐστὶν ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ (“Jesus is the Son of God”) Macro Observation
Love moves from definition to completion. God’s love is said to be τετελειωμένη (“brought to completion”) in us, not by us. Confession and abiding function together as visible criteria of divine presence. Diagnostic Function
Love is shown as relationally completed, not merely acknowledged. The cycle ties confession, abiding, and love into a single evidentiary unit. Cycle 14 — Love Versus Fear 1 John 4:17–18
Textual Focus
Contrast: ἀγάπη (“love”) vs φόβος (“fear”) Judgment language: ἡμέρα τῆς κρίσεως (“day of judgment”) Macro Observation
Love and fear are presented as mutually exclusive realms. Fear is associated with κόλασις (“punishment”). Perfected love results in boldness, not anxiety. Diagnostic Function
This cycle diagnoses fear as a signal of incomplete love, not as a motivational tool. Love’s function is stability before judgment, not emotional reassurance. Cycle 15 — Final Diagnostic: Love, Belief, and Testimony 1 John 4:19 – 5:21
Textual Focus
Repeated markers: Love/hate contrast Belief in the Son Testimony language (μαρτυρία – “testimony”) Life/death contrast Macro Observation
The final cycle integrates all prior diagnostics: Love for God ↔ love for brother Belief ↔ possession of life Acceptance of testimony ↔ alignment with God Hatred, false belief, and denial are shown as source indicators, not isolated sins. Diagnostic Function
This cycle functions as a comprehensive verification pass. The letter closes by re-asserting source clarity (“from God” vs “from the world”) and issuing a final warning against false objects of trust. Macro Summary of Cycles 11–15 Love is: From God (Cycle 11) Defined by God’s action (Cycle 12) Completed through abiding and confession (Cycle 13) Incompatible with fear (Cycle 14) Verified through belief, testimony, and relational consistency (Cycle 15) Functionally: These cycles do not command love to create life They diagnose source by manifested love

body: ΙΩΑΝΝΟΥ Α 1John 1:1	Ὃ ἦν ἀπʼ ἀρχῆς, ὃ ἀκηκόαμεν, ὃ ἑωράκαμεν τοῖς ὀφθαλμοῖς ἡμῶν, ὃ ἐθεασάμεθα καὶ αἱ χεῖρες ἡμῶν ἐψηλάφησαν, περὶ τοῦ λόγου τῆς ζωῆς— 1John 1:2	καὶ ἡ ζωὴ ἐφανερώθη, καὶ ἑωράκαμεν καὶ μαρτυροῦμεν καὶ ἀπαγγέλλομεν ὑμῖν τὴν ζωὴν τὴν αἰώνιον ἥτις ἦν πρὸς τὸν πατέρα καὶ ἐφανερώθη ἡμῖν— 1John 1:3	ὃ ἑωράκαμεν καὶ ἀκηκόαμεν ἀπαγγέλλομεν ⸀καὶ ὑμῖν, ἵνα καὶ ὑμεῖς κοινωνίαν ἔχητε μεθʼ ἡμῶν· καὶ ἡ κοινωνία δὲ ἡ ἡμετέρα μετὰ τοῦ πατρὸς καὶ μετὰ τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ· 1John 1:4	καὶ ταῦτα γράφομεν ⸀ἡμεῖς ἵνα ἡ χαρὰ ἡμῶν ᾖ πεπληρωμένη. 1John 1:5	Καὶ ἔστιν αὕτη ἡ ἀγγελία ἣν ἀκηκόαμεν ἀπʼ αὐτοῦ καὶ ἀναγγέλλομεν ὑμῖν, ὅτι ὁ θεὸς φῶς ἐστιν καὶ σκοτία ⸂ἐν αὐτῷ οὐκ ἔστιν⸃ οὐδεμία. 1John 1:6	ἐὰν εἴπωμεν ὅτι κοινωνίαν ἔχομεν μετʼ αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐν τῷ σκότει περιπατῶμεν, 1John 5:6	Οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἐλθὼν διʼ ὕδατος καὶ αἵματος, Ἰησοῦς Χριστός· οὐκ ἐν τῷ ὕδατι μόνον ἀλλʼ ἐν τῷ ὕδατι καὶ ⸀ἐν τῷ αἵματι· καὶ τὸ πνεῦμά ἐστιν τὸ μαρτυροῦν, ὅτι τὸ πνεῦμά ἐστιν ἡ ἀλήθεια. 1John 5:7	ὅτι τρεῖς εἰσιν οἱ μαρτυροῦντες, 1John 5:8	τὸ πνεῦμα καὶ τὸ ὕδωρ καὶ τὸ αἷμα, καὶ οἱ τρεῖς εἰς τὸ ἕν εἰσιν. 1John 5:9	εἰ τὴν μαρτυρίαν τῶν ἀνθρώπων λαμβάνομεν, ἡ μαρτυρία τοῦ θεοῦ μείζων ἐστίν, ὅτι αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ μαρτυρία τοῦ θεοῦ ⸀ὅτι μεμαρτύρηκεν περὶ τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ. 1John 5:10	ὁ πιστεύων εἰς τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ θεοῦ ἔχει τὴν μαρτυρίαν ἐν ⸀αὑτῷ· ὁ μὴ πιστεύων τῷ θεῷ ψεύστην πεποίηκεν αὐτόν, ὅτι οὐ πεπίστευκεν εἰς τὴν μαρτυρίαν ἣν μεμαρτύρηκεν ὁ θεὸς περὶ τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ. 1John 5:11	καὶ αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ μαρτυρία, ὅτι ζωὴν αἰώνιον ἔδωκεν ⸂ὁ θεὸς ἡμῖν⸃, καὶ αὕτη ἡ ζωὴ ἐν τῷ υἱῷ αὐτοῦ ἐστιν. 1John 5:12	ὁ ἔχων τὸν υἱὸν ἔχει τὴν ζωήν· ὁ μὴ ἔχων τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ θεοῦ τὴν ζωὴν οὐκ ἔχει. 1John 5:13	Ταῦτα ἔγραψα ⸀ὑμῖν ἵνα εἰδῆτε ὅτι ζωὴν ⸂ἔχετε αἰώνιον⸃, ⸂τοῖς πιστεύουσιν⸃ εἰς τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ θεοῦ. 1John 5:14	καὶ αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ παρρησία ἣν ἔχομεν πρὸς αὐτόν, ὅτι ἐάν τι αἰτώμεθα κατὰ τὸ θέλημα αὐτοῦ ἀκούει ἡμῶν. 1John 5:15	καὶ ἐὰν οἴδαμεν ὅτι ἀκούει ἡμῶν ὃ ⸀ἐὰν αἰτώμεθα, οἴδαμεν ὅτι ἔχομεν τὰ αἰτήματα ἃ ᾐτήκαμεν ⸀ἀπʼ αὐτοῦ. 1John 5:16	ἐάν τις ἴδῃ τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ ἁμαρτάνοντα ἁμαρτίαν μὴ πρὸς θάνατον, αἰτήσει, καὶ δώσει αὐτῷ ζωήν, τοῖς ἁμαρτάνουσιν μὴ πρὸς θάνατον. ἔστιν ἁμαρτία πρὸς θάνατον· οὐ περὶ ἐκείνης λέγω ἵνα ἐρωτήσῃ. 1John 5:17	πᾶσα ἀδικία ἁμαρτία ἐστίν, καὶ ἔστιν ἁμαρτία οὐ πρὸς θάνατον. 1John 5:18	Οἴδαμεν ὅτι πᾶς ὁ γεγεννημένος ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ οὐχ ἁμαρτάνει, ἀλλʼ ὁ γεννηθεὶς ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ τηρεῖ ⸀αὐτόν, καὶ ὁ πονηρὸς οὐχ ἅπτεται αὐτοῦ. 1John 5:19	οἴδαμεν ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐσμεν, καὶ ὁ κόσμος ὅλος ἐν τῷ πονηρῷ κεῖται. 1John 5:20	οἴδαμεν δὲ ὅτι ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ ἥκει, καὶ δέδωκεν ἡμῖν διάνοιαν ἵνα ⸀γινώσκωμεν τὸν ἀληθινόν· καὶ ἐσμὲν ἐν τῷ ἀληθινῷ, ἐν τῷ υἱῷ αὐτοῦ Ἰησοῦ Χριστῷ. οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἀληθινὸς θεὸς καὶ ζωὴ αἰώνιος. 1John 5:21	Τεκνία, φυλάξατε ἑαυτὰ ἀπὸ τῶν ⸀εἰδώλων.
